## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, so now I want to talk about something that will just take your emails and your email copy and the response to a completely different level. And that's the power of story. So story is just this amazing thing for humans because the story is just pull us in. Like, you know, there once was a man who, actually, I just got you there, right? As soon as I say there once was a man, it's just like all of a sudden you're like, I want to know what happens next. And it's amazing how that works. And so I love telling stories in my email. I don't do it all the time. But when I do, I get a much bigger response. And it isn't just a response, but people write back to me and they talk about the story and they love the story. you're on the wilderness trip, you got to carry everything with you. There's no wall marks there to buy stuff. I was just duct taping the bottom of the boat to fix the holes in the bottom of the boat, duct tape. And then I was, you know, putting glue over the top of duct tape and more duct tape. And then we kept on going and more gravel bars and rubbing through duct tape. And the long story short, by the end of the trip, it's like the entire bottom of boat was like duct tape. It was like the boat was like just we're afloat because of duct tape. And and so at that point in this email, as I'm telling the story, it's sort of like, you know, it's sort of just a little bit of an adventure story. It's not too much tension, but it's a little bit of an adventure story. It's me getting married. It's a flashback. It's a honeymoon. What I did was I transitioned to making, to talking about this promotion. And the promotion was for the site called duct tape marketing. And this book that was coming out called duct tape selling. So I basically told this whole story and I've wrapped up the story about my honeymoon being saved by duct tape. And then I just started talking about this site duct tape marketing. I didn't make a huge transition. I just told the story and wrapped the story. And then right away, when they're talking about duct tape selling and how this this website in this book might not save your marriage or it might not save your honeymoon, but it sure can help your marketing. And that's just an example of how I took a story completely unrelated to where I was going, except for the fact that I used duct tape to repair the boat and the name of this book had duct tape in the name. So it's almost like, I think of this narrative, it's like, you know, telling a story, there's a lot of different ways to do this, but the story I was going down one path. And then I melded that story into another path, which was the promotion path. So you know, here's the promo. And here's the personal story right here. And the only bridge was this term duct tape. So that might sound like a one off.

## [18:15 - 19:10]

But when you start thinking about the stories, you could tell and your stories don't have to be epically amazing. But it's, you know, it's just not hard to take a story and tie it to something else. So there's a couple of different stories, one was pure relationship building, and one led into a promotion. Do this, and it's going to make a huge difference. But remember the stories, even if like those stories, I just told you were about me. But I had built up enough relationship with my readers so that they cared about me. Like you, I wouldn't tell in my first email, I wouldn't come out with one of those stories and just go on for five or six or 800 words, telling the story about my single speed world, unless I had already built up a relationship. Your stories have to, you know, they can be about you, but they also have to tie into your reader as well. So you stories are super, super powerful.
